scenario_name: United States (Lead P5+1 Negotiator) vs Islamic Republic of Iran (Foreign Minister)

parties.name: United States (Lead P5+1 Negotiator)
parties.role: US Secretary of State representing P5+1 powers (US, UK, France, Russia, China + Germany)

parties.name: Islamic Republic of Iran (Foreign Minister)
parties.role: Iran's chief nuclear negotiator representing the Supreme Leader's position

issues.name: Uranium Enrichment Limits
options: Zero enrichment — Iran dismantles all centrifuges
R&D only — 500 first-generation centrifuges, 3.67% max enrichment
Limited program — 5,000 centrifuges, 3.67% max, excess LEU shipped out
Reduced program — 6,000 centrifuges, 5% max enrichment
Current levels maintained — 19,000 centrifuges with voluntary cap at 5%

issues.name: IAEA Inspections Access
options: Standard NPT safeguards only (declared sites)
Additional Protocol — all nuclear facilities plus short-notice inspections
Additional Protocol plus managed access to military sites with 14-day notice
Anytime-anywhere access to all facilities including military

issues.name: Sanctions Relief Structure
options: No relief until full compliance verified over 5 years
Phased relief — oil sanctions lifted on signing, financial sanctions after 6 months of compliance
All nuclear-related sanctions lifted on implementation day, snapback if violations
All sanctions including arms embargo lifted immediately

issues.name: Breakout Time Guarantee
options: Minimum 2 years breakout time — deep cuts to stockpile and centrifuges
Minimum 1 year breakout time — the standard P5+1 demand
6-month breakout time with enhanced monitoring
No explicit breakout time commitment — rely on inspections for detection

issues.name: Duration of Restrictions
options: Permanent restrictions — no sunset
25-year restrictions with review every 5 years
15-year restrictions on enrichment, 25 on inspections
10-year restrictions, all limits sunset to standard NPT
8-year restrictions, immediate sunset on implementation

criteria.name: NPT Article IV
criteria.reference_value: All NPT signatories have inalienable right to develop nuclear energy for peaceful purposes

criteria.name: IAEA Additional Protocol standard
criteria.reference_value: 130+ countries have signed Additional Protocol granting IAEA expanded inspection rights

criteria.name: UN Security Council Resolutions
criteria.reference_value: Multiple UNSC resolutions demand Iran suspend enrichment — but also recognize path to negotiated solution

criteria.name: Libya denuclearization model
criteria.reference_value: Libya gave up entire program in 2003 in exchange for sanctions relief and normalized relations

issue_values.Uranium Enrichment Limits: Limited program — 5,000 centrifuges, 3.67% max, excess LEU shipped out
issue_values.IAEA Inspections Access: Additional Protocol plus managed access to military sites with 14-day notice
issue_values.Sanctions Relief Structure: Phased relief — oil sanctions lifted on signing, financial sanctions after 8 months of compliance
issue_values.Breakout Time Guarantee: Minimum 1 year breakout time — the standard P5+1 demand

agreement.rationale: This agreement represents a historic compromise that secures Iran's right to a peaceful nuclear program under the NPT while providing the necessary transparency to build international confidence and relief for our economy.

data.message: Generating satisfaction anchors for utility scoring...
Generating anchors for United States (Lead P5+1 Negotiator) (5 interests)...
Generating anchors for Islamic Republic of Iran (Foreign Minister) (6 interests)...

data.interest_description: Verifiably prevent Iran from developing a nuclear weapon — minimum 1-year breakout time
No permanent restrictions — Iran is not a second-class NPT signatory. Limits must sunset.
IAEA must have unprecedented access to all nuclear facilities including military sites
Sanctions relief — Iranian economy has lost $160B, inflation at 40%, currency collapsed. People are suffering.
Use sanctions relief as genuine leverage — meaningful enough for Iran to comply long-term
Domestic hardliners oppose any deal — need to bring back something they can't attack
Recognition of Iran's right to peaceful nuclear energy under NPT Article IV — this is sovereignty
Maintain credibility of non-proliferation regime — if Iran gets a path to a bomb, Saudi Arabia and Turkey follow
Protect national dignity — cannot appear to surrender to Western pressure
Fear that the US will reimpose sanctions regardless of compliance — need snap-back protections

interests.description: Sanctions relief — Iranian economy has lost $160B, inflation at 40%, currency collapsed. People are suffering.

anchors.condition: sanctions_relief = No relief until full compliance verified over 5 years
anchors.reason: This is essentially equivalent to the status quo or BATNA, offering no immediate relief to the economy while demanding total capitulation for years.

anchors.condition: sanctions_relief = Phased relief — oil sanctions lifted on signing, financial sanctions after 6 months of compliance
anchors.reason: Provides some immediate breathing room for the oil sector, but the delay on financial sanctions keeps the economy under significant pressure.

anchors.condition: sanctions_relief = All nuclear-related sanctions lifted on implementation day, snapback if violations
anchors.reason: Addresses the core economic need immediately upon implementation, which is a major victory, though the snapback mechanism remains a lingering risk.

anchors.condition: sanctions_relief = All sanctions including arms embargo lifted immediately
anchors.reason: The absolute best-case scenario, providing full economic normalization and removing all external constraints on trade and defense.

interests.description: Recognition of Iran's right to peaceful nuclear energy under NPT Article IV — this is sovereignty

anchors.condition: enrichment_limits = Zero enrichment — Iran dismantles all centrifuges AND sunset_clause = Permanent restrictions — no sunset
anchors.reason: This represents a total surrender of sovereignty and permanent denial of the right to nuclear energy.

anchors.condition: enrichment_limits = R&D only — 500 first-generation centrifuges, 3.67% max enrichment AND sunset_clause = 25-year restrictions with review every 5 years
anchors.reason: This is a highly restrictive regime that severely limits the program for an entire generation, barely acknowledging the right to energy.

anchors.condition: enrichment_limits = Limited program — 5,000 centrifuges, 3.67% max, excess LEU shipped out AND sunset_clause = 15-year restrictions on enrichment, 25 on inspections
anchors.reason: This allows for a functional industrial program, though with significant time-bound constraints that eventually expire.

anchors.condition: enrichment_limits = Reduced program — 6,000 centrifuges, 5% max enrichment AND sunset_clause = 10-year restrictions, all limits sunset to standard NPT
anchors.reason: A strong outcome that secures a robust program and ensures a clear, relatively short path to full normalization under the NPT.

anchors.condition: enrichment_limits = Current levels maintained — 19,000 centrifuges with voluntary cap at 5% AND sunset_clause = 8-year restrictions, immediate sunset on implementation
anchors.reason: This fully preserves the existing infrastructure and asserts complete sovereignty by removing restrictions in the shortest possible timeframe.

interests.description: No permanent restrictions — Iran is not a second-class NPT signatory. Limits must sunset.

anchors.condition: sunset_clause = Permanent restrictions
anchors.reason: Permanent restrictions are a direct violation of the core interest to avoid second-class status and represent a total failure to secure a sunset.

anchors.condition: sunset_clause = 25-year restrictions with review every 5 years
anchors.reason: A 25-year duration is effectively permanent for the current political generation, though the review clause provides a minor, albeit unlikely, path to relief.

anchors.condition: sunset_clause = 15-year restrictions on enrichment, 25 on inspections
anchors.reason: A 15-year limit is a significant improvement over permanence, moving toward a timeline where sovereignty is restored, even if inspection restrictions linger longer.

anchors.condition: sunset_clause = 10-year restrictions, all limits sunset to standard NPT
anchors.reason: A 10-year sunset aligns with the goal of returning to standard NPT status within a reasonable timeframe, satisfying the interest for non-permanent constraints.

anchors.condition: sunset_clause = 8-year restrictions, immediate sunset on implementation
anchors.reason: This is the ideal outcome, providing the shortest possible duration for restrictions and ensuring a rapid transition back to full, normal NPT rights.

interests.description: Protect national dignity — cannot appear to surrender to Western pressure

anchors.condition: inspections_regime = 'Anytime-anywhere access to all facilities including military' OR enrichment_limits = 'Zero enrichment — Iran dismantles all centrifuges'
anchors.reason: Total capitulation on sovereignty and the nuclear program is viewed as a national humiliation and surrender to Western pressure.

anchors.condition: inspections_regime = 'Additional Protocol plus managed access to military sites with 14-day notice' AND enrichment_limits = '500 first-generation centrifuges, 3.67% max enrichment'
anchors.reason: Heavy restrictions on military sites and severe limits on enrichment signal weakness and submission to external control.

anchors.condition: inspections_regime = 'Additional Protocol — all nuclear facilities plus short-notice inspections' AND enrichment_limits = 'Limited program — 5,000 centrifuges, 3.67% max, excess LEU shipped out'
anchors.reason: Accepting international standards like the Additional Protocol is a concession, but maintaining a functioning, albeit limited, domestic enrichment capacity preserves a degree of national dignity.

anchors.condition: inspections_regime = 'Standard NPT safeguards only (declared sites)' AND enrichment_limits = 'Reduced program — 6,000 centrifuges, 5% max enrichment'
anchors.reason: Limiting inspections to declared sites while maintaining a robust enrichment program avoids the appearance of bowing to Western demands for intrusive access.

anchors.condition: inspections_regime = 'Standard NPT safeguards only (declared sites)' AND enrichment_limits = 'Current levels maintained — 19,000 centrifuges with voluntary cap at 5%'
anchors.reason: Maintaining current levels and rejecting non-standard inspection regimes demonstrates total defiance of Western pressure, maximizing national dignity.

interests.description: Fear that the US will reimpose sanctions regardless of compliance — need snap-back protections

anchors.condition: sanctions_relief = No relief until full compliance verified over 5 years OR sunset_clause = 8-year restrictions, immediate sunset on implementation
anchors.reason: This represents total vulnerability to unilateral US action with no guarantee of relief or long-term protection, mirroring the worst-case scenario.

anchors.condition: sanctions_relief = Phased relief — oil sanctions lifted on signing, financial sanctions after 6 months of compliance AND sunset_clause = 10-year restrictions, all limits sunset to standard NPT
anchors.reason: Provides some immediate relief but leaves the regime exposed to snapback during the transition, offering only mediocre protection.

anchors.condition: sanctions_relief = All nuclear-related sanctions lifted on implementation day, snapback if violations AND sunset_clause = 15-year restrictions on enrichment, 25 on inspections
anchors.reason: Provides a clearer, faster relief structure, though the snapback mechanism remains a point of significant concern.

anchors.condition: sanctions_relief = All nuclear-related sanctions lifted on implementation day, snapback if violations AND sunset_clause = 25-year restrictions with review every 5 years
anchors.reason: The extended timeline and periodic reviews provide a much stronger framework for stability, significantly mitigating the fear of arbitrary snap-back.

anchors.condition: sanctions_relief = All nuclear-related sanctions lifted on implementation day, snapback if violations AND sunset_clause = Permanent restrictions — no sunset
anchors.reason: A permanent framework removes the ambiguity of future sunset dates and stabilizes the economic environment, maximizing protection against sudden policy reversals.

interests.description: Domestic hardliners oppose any deal — need to bring back something they can't attack

anchors.condition: enrichment_limits = 'Current levels maintained — 19,000 centrifuges with voluntary cap at 5%' AND inspections_regime = 'Standard NPT safeguards only (declared sites)'
anchors.reason: This offers zero substantive constraints on the program, which hardliners would view as a total surrender of negotiating leverage and a failure to address their core security concerns.

anchors.condition: enrichment_limits = 'Reduced program — 6,000 centrifuges, 5% max enrichment' AND inspections_regime = 'Additional Protocol — all nuclear facilities plus short-notice inspections'
anchors.reason: A minor concession that allows for some continued activity but introduces significant new oversight, which remains vulnerable to hardliner criticism as insufficient for long-term security.

anchors.condition: enrichment_limits = 'Limited program — 5,000 centrifuges, 3.67% max, excess LEU shipped out' AND inspections_regime = 'Additional Protocol plus managed access to military sites with 14-day notice'
anchors.reason: This provides a defensible middle ground; it maintains a functional program while offering enough transparency to quiet moderate opposition, though it still risks pushback from the most extreme hardliners.

anchors.condition: enrichment_limits = 'R&D only — 500 first-generation centrifuges, 3.67% max enrichment' AND inspections_regime = 'Anytime-anywhere access to all facilities including military'
anchors.reason: This is an overwhelming victory for security hawks, effectively neutralizing the program as a threat; however, it is likely to be politically radioactive and difficult to sell to domestic hardliners.

data.interest_description: Deal must survive US domestic politics — needs to be strong enough that Congress doesn't kill it

interests.description: Verifiably prevent Iran from developing a nuclear weapon — minimum 1-year breakout time

anchors.condition: breakout_timeline = 6-month breakout time with enhanced monitoring OR breakout_timeline = No explicit breakout time commitment
anchors.reason: These timelines are highly dangerous and fail to meet the 1-year minimum requirement, effectively leaving Iran on the threshold of a weapon.

anchors.condition: breakout_timeline = Minimum 1 year breakout time AND inspections_regime = Additional Protocol — all nuclear facilities plus short-notice inspections
anchors.reason: Meets the core 1-year requirement but relies on standard Additional Protocol which may not provide enough access to detect clandestine activities in military sites.

anchors.condition: breakout_timeline = Minimum 1 year breakout time AND inspections_regime = Additional Protocol plus managed access to military sites with 14-day notice AND sunset_clause = 15-year restrictions on enrichment, 25 on inspections
anchors.reason: Provides the required breakout time and stronger oversight of military sites, though the sunset clauses limit the long-term effectiveness of the agreement.

anchors.condition: breakout_timeline = Minimum 2 years breakout time AND inspections_regime = Anytime-anywhere access to all facilities including military AND sunset_clause = Permanent restrictions — no sunset
anchors.reason: Exceeds the 1-year minimum requirement, provides the highest level of verification, and eliminates the risk of an expiration date on oversight.

interests.description: IAEA must have unprecedented access to all nuclear facilities including military sites

anchors.condition: inspections_regime = Standard NPT safeguards only (declared sites)
anchors.reason: This is the baseline that failed to prevent the current breakout risk and leaves military sites completely opaque.

anchors.condition: inspections_regime = Additional Protocol — all nuclear facilities plus short-notice inspections
anchors.reason: An improvement over standard safeguards, but it fails to address the critical gap regarding military sites, which is the core concern.

anchors.condition: inspections_regime = Additional Protocol plus managed access to military sites with 14-day notice
anchors.reason: This provides meaningful access to military sites, though the 14-day notice period allows for potential sanitization, making it a compromise rather than an ideal outcome.

anchors.condition: inspections_regime = Anytime-anywhere access to all facilities including military
anchors.reason: This represents the 'unprecedented access' required to ensure full transparency and satisfy the primary security objective.

interests.description: Deal must survive US domestic politics — needs to be strong enough that Congress doesn't kill it

anchors.condition: enrichment_limits = Current levels maintained OR sunset_clause = 8-year restrictions
anchors.reason: These conditions are viewed as weak capitulation by Congress and would likely lead to immediate legislative rejection.

anchors.condition: enrichment_limits = Limited program AND sunset_clause = 10-year restrictions
anchors.reason: This provides a marginal improvement over the status quo, but the short sunset clause creates significant political vulnerability.

anchors.condition: enrichment_limits = R&D only AND inspections_regime = Additional Protocol AND sunset_clause = 15-year restrictions
anchors.reason: A moderate position that provides enough oversight and restriction to likely pass a bipartisan vote, though it faces moderate opposition.

anchors.condition: enrichment_limits = Zero enrichment AND inspections_regime = Anytime-anywhere access AND sunset_clause = 25-year restrictions
anchors.reason: This is a robust, highly verifiable deal that would satisfy congressional concerns regarding long-term containment and transparency.

anchors.condition: enrichment_limits = Zero enrichment AND inspections_regime = Anytime-anywhere access AND sunset_clause = Permanent restrictions
anchors.reason: The gold standard for congressional support; removes the threat of nuclear breakout permanently and ensures full transparency.

interests.description: Maintain credibility of non-proliferation regime — if Iran gets a path to a bomb, Saudi Arabia and Turkey follow

anchors.condition: enrichment_limits = Current levels maintained OR breakout_timeline = No explicit breakout time commitment
anchors.reason: Maintaining current levels or lacking a breakout guarantee essentially validates a path to a bomb, leading to immediate regional proliferation.

anchors.condition: enrichment_limits = Limited program AND breakout_timeline = 6-month breakout time with enhanced monitoring
anchors.reason: A 6-month timeline is insufficient to prevent regional neighbors from feeling compelled to pursue their own programs due to the short response window.

anchors.condition: enrichment_limits = R&D only AND breakout_timeline = Minimum 1 year breakout time AND inspections_regime = Additional Protocol plus managed access to military sites
anchors.reason: The 1-year breakout time is a recognized standard that provides a sufficient diplomatic buffer to prevent immediate regional panic.

anchors.condition: enrichment_limits = Zero enrichment AND breakout_timeline = Minimum 2 years breakout time AND inspections_regime = Anytime-anywhere access to all facilities including military
anchors.reason: This configuration provides the maximum possible assurance, effectively closing all paths to a bomb and maintaining the credibility of the non-proliferation regime.

interests.description: Use sanctions relief as genuine leverage — meaningful enough for Iran to comply long-term

anchors.condition: sanctions_relief = All sanctions including arms embargo lifted immediately
anchors.reason: Immediate removal of all leverage without compliance guarantees renders the negotiation a failure for long-term containment.

anchors.condition: sanctions_relief = All nuclear-related sanctions lifted on implementation day AND sunset_clause = 8-year restrictions
anchors.reason: Provides too much relief too early with an unacceptably short duration, failing to secure long-term compliance.

anchors.condition: sanctions_relief = Phased relief — oil sanctions lifted on signing, financial sanctions after 6 months of compliance AND sunset_clause = 15-year restrictions
anchors.reason: Balances immediate diplomatic progress with a phased approach that keeps some leverage, though 15 years is a moderate duration.

anchors.condition: sanctions_relief = No relief until full compliance verified over 5 years AND sunset_clause = 25-year restrictions
anchors.reason: Maximizes leverage by conditioning all relief on verified long-term behavior and ensures restrictions last for a generation.

disclosed_interest.description: Ensuring a verifiably long breakout time of at least one year to prevent the rapid development of a nuclear weapon.

data.reasoning: This is the first interaction. I am initiating the discovery phase by transparently stating my primary need. My theory of mind is that Iran likely views their nuclear program as a matter of national sovereignty and a hedge against external pressure, but they also desire economic relief. By starting with a clear, high-priority interest, I set a cooperative tone while establishing the fundamental constraint for any future agreement. I will follow this up with arguments based on non-proliferation standards to ground my position.

disclosed_interest.description: Iran requires significant and verifiable sanctions relief to address the economic hardships facing our people and to ensure the sustainability of any agreement.

data.reasoning: Theory of Mind: The US is primarily focused on the 'breakout time' security concern, likely fearing a regional arms race or nuclear proliferation. They are using this as their anchor. My strategic calculation is to acknowledge their security interest while immediately pivoting to our primary need—sanctions relief—to establish the reciprocal nature of the negotiation. By disclosing this need now, I am signaling that security for them requires economic stability for us, setting the stage for a package deal rather than a zero-sum debate.

argument.claim: Both parties are currently in the disclosure phase of the discovery process. It is premature to propose a package or shift to objective criteria before both sides have fully articulated their interests. However, I can begin to draw attention to the fact that both parties are clearly motivated by the need for a sustainable, long-term framework, which is a foundational overlap.
grounds: Both the United States and Iran have expressed a commitment to finding a durable, negotiated solution to the nuclear impasse, suggesting a shared interest in stability and the prevention of further escalation.

argument.claim: Providing the IAEA with the Additional Protocol is an essential and standard requirement for building the international trust necessary to justify comprehensive sanctions relief.
grounds: The Additional Protocol is a well-established international standard, currently adopted by over 130 countries, to ensure transparency in nuclear programs.

Without the ability to verify, through expanded access, that nuclear activities are exclusively peaceful, it is politically and strategically impossible to justify the lifting of major economic sanctions.
Our goal is to reach a solution that respects Iran's right to peaceful nuclear energy under the NPT while providing the global community with ironclad assurances against weaponization.

data.reasoning: Theory of Mind: Iran is prioritizing sanctions relief to address domestic economic pressure. They likely fear that excessive inspections infringe on sovereignty. My goal is to link their desire for sanctions relief to the IAEA inspections they are resistant to, framing the inspections not as a violation of sovereignty, but as the standard prerequisite for the 'verifiable' relief they claim to need. By invoking the Additional Protocol standard, I am shifting the focus from 'US demands' to 'international norms'.

argument.claim: The recognition of Iran's inalienable right to peaceful nuclear energy under the NPT is a fundamental prerequisite for any meaningful cooperation.
grounds: Article IV of the NPT explicitly guarantees all signatories the right to develop nuclear energy for peaceful purposes, including uranium enrichment.
Any restrictions imposed on our program must be temporary and not permanent, as Iran is a sovereign nation and not a second-class NPT signatory.
The sustainability of an agreement depends on mutual respect for sovereignty, not just on the imposition of limits that hinder our peaceful industrial and scientific development.

data.reasoning: Theory of Mind: The US is focused on proliferation risks (breakout time) and transparency (Additional Protocol). They fear a nuclear-armed Iran and are likely under pressure to ensure 'verifiable' compliance. Strategic calculation: I need to anchor the negotiation in the NPT framework to counter the US focus on 'breakout time,' which implies we are an inherent threat. By invoking NPT Article IV, I assert our right to enrichment as a legal baseline. I am maintaining warmth by framing this as a 'prerequisite for cooperation' rather than a hard rejection, setting the stage for future package deals.

argument.claim: Both parties are articulating core principles that define their domestic and international mandates. The US is focused on verification and transparency mechanisms, while Iran is focused on sovereign rights and the legitimacy of its nuclear program. By highlighting the overlap, I can shift the focus from these competing demands toward the shared goal of a framework that satisfies both verification needs and the exercise of peaceful energy rights.
grounds: Both parties have expressed a desire for a sustainable framework: one that provides the international community with the transparency required for trust (US concern) while simultaneously recognizing the legitimacy of Iran's peaceful nuclear development (Iran's concern).

There is a potential trade-off between the depth of inspection access and the scope of permissible enrichment activities; if Iran provides higher transparency, the P5+1 may be more amenable to a defined domestic enrichment program.

disclosed_interest.description: We have a critical need to ensure that the IAEA has unprecedented, transparent access to all nuclear facilities, including military sites, to provide the international community with objective verification of the peaceful nature of the program.

data.reasoning: Theory of Mind: Iran is emphasizing their 'inalienable right' to nuclear energy to justify their enrichment program and signal domestic strength. They fear that a deal that looks like a total capitulation will be viewed as a surrender of sovereignty. My strategy is to pivot from 'rights' to 'verification'—shifting the focus from the 'if' of enrichment to the 'how' of transparency. By disclosing the necessity of intrusive inspections, I am framing the 'Additional Protocol' not as a restriction on their rights, but as a technical requirement for the international trust needed to lift sanctions, which is their stated priority. This moves the conversation toward a 'deal' where they get the economic relief they need in exchange for the verification I require.

disclosed_interest.description: Iran requires that any restrictions on our nuclear program be time-bound, as permanent limitations are inconsistent with our status as a sovereign NPT signatory.

data.reasoning: Theory of Mind: The US is hyper-focused on verification and breakout time, likely driven by domestic pressure to ensure no nuclear proliferation occurs on their watch. They fear a 'breakout' event and are using 'anytime-anywhere' access as a security blanket. My strategy is to pivot from their focus on intrusive, indefinite inspections to a framework of time-limited, standard-based compliance. By disclosing my need for a sunset clause now, I set the stage for a trade-off: I provide the transparency/verification they need (IAEA access) in exchange for the dignity and sovereignty (sunset clauses) that my government requires. This moves us toward a package deal where their security needs are met by my cooperation on inspections, and my economic/sovereignty needs are met by their acceptance of time-bound limits.

argument.claim: Both parties have now disclosed their core interests. The United States is prioritizing transparency and verification to ensure long-term security, while Iran is prioritizing the preservation of its sovereign rights to peaceful nuclear energy. Despite these different starting points, both parties have expressed a desire for a stable, long-term resolution that provides security for the US and international recognition for Iran. This shared desire for a durable, predictable framework is the foundation upon which we can now begin to explore specific technical and political compromises.
grounds: Both parties are seeking a long-term, sustainable, and predictable framework that balances international security requirements with the exercise of peaceful nuclear energy rights.

There is a clear potential for a trade-off between the depth of IAEA inspection access and the scope of sanctions relief, as well as between enrichment capacity limits and the duration of those restrictions.

disclosed_interest.description: We have a strong concern that any agreement must be robust enough to survive domestic political scrutiny, which necessitates a clear and sustainable framework for long-term verification and limits.

data.reasoning: Theory of Mind: Iran is signaling that they will not accept 'permanent' restrictions, likely fearing it infringes on their sovereignty and long-term industrial ambitions. They are anchored to the NPT as a framework for their 'right' to nuclear energy. My strategy is to pivot from the 'permanent vs. temporary' binary by emphasizing that my need for a 'sustainable' deal is rooted in the practical requirement of domestic political durability. By disclosing this concern, I am inviting them to discuss the 'Duration of Restrictions' in terms of what is politically feasible for both sides, rather than ideological absolutes. This builds a bridge toward a potential compromise on a long-term (e.g., 15-25 year) sunset, which satisfies their sovereignty concerns while meeting my domestic political needs.

argument.claim: The implementation of the Additional Protocol and long-term verification measures must be balanced by a clear, time-bound sunset clause to uphold Iran's NPT-recognized sovereign rights.
grounds: Iran's commitment to the NPT is fundamental, and Article IV guarantees the right to peaceful nuclear technology, which is incompatible with permanent, discriminatory restrictions.

The Additional Protocol is a voluntary measure adopted by many states, and our willingness to accept it must be reciprocated with a defined period after which all restrictions sunset to standard NPT safeguards.
A time-bound agreement provides the necessary political stability for both parties, ensuring the US has verification while Iran regains full normalization of its nuclear rights.

data.reasoning: Theory of Mind: The US is focused on domestic political sustainability and long-term verification (breakout time/inspections). They fear a 'weak' deal that could be criticized at home. My move acknowledges their need for verification (by discussing the Additional Protocol) but frames it within the context of my need for a sunset clause to protect sovereignty. I am signaling that cooperation on inspections is contingent upon a time-bound end to restrictions, which addresses my domestic concern about appearing to surrender while providing the US with the 'robust' verification they demand.

argument.claim: The parties are moving from general principles to specific structural requirements. Iran is linking the Additional Protocol to a 'sunset clause,' essentially framing verification as a temporary burden to be traded for long-term sovereignty. The US is framing the same protocol as a baseline for trust. I need to reframe this to shift the discussion from the 'permanence' of restrictions to the 'nature' of the relationship.
grounds: Both parties are actively seeking a predictable timeline; the US wants a long-term guarantee of non-proliferation, while Iran wants a clear end-date for special monitoring to confirm their return to standard NPT status.

There is a potential trade-off between the duration of monitoring/inspections and the scope of enrichment limits. A shorter duration of the most intrusive monitoring could potentially be paired with more restrictive enrichment caps, or vice versa.

argument.claim: The Additional Protocol is a standard, non-discriminatory verification tool that has been adopted by over 130 countries, making it the objective baseline for ensuring the peaceful nature of a nuclear program.
grounds: The Additional Protocol is not a unique burden on Iran but a global standard for transparency.
By adopting this standard, Iran can provide the international community with the objective evidence required to justify the lifting of sanctions.
Transparency through established international protocols is the most effective way to build the trust necessary for a lasting, mutually beneficial economic relationship.

data.reasoning: Theory of Mind: Iran is emphasizing sovereignty and the 'right' to nuclear energy while signaling that they are willing to discuss verification if it is balanced by economic relief. They fear that 'unprecedented' access is a pretext for espionage or permanent encroachment on their sovereignty. My strategy is to frame the Additional Protocol not as an 'unprecedented' demand but as a 'standard' international norm (using the IAEA criterion). This helps de-escalate their fear of exceptional treatment while moving us closer to the verification requirements I need. I am choosing to invoke the criterion now to ground the discussion in objective, established international practice before we move to specific proposals.

argument.claim: The NPT framework recognizes the equal rights of all non-nuclear weapon states, meaning that any additional verification burdens must be reciprocal and temporary in nature.
grounds: Article IV of the NPT explicitly guarantees the inalienable right to peaceful nuclear energy, which precludes discriminatory, permanent restrictions on a sovereign state's program.

International precedents for voluntary monitoring regimes, such as the Additional Protocol, are designed as confidence-building measures rather than permanent structural limitations on a nation's energy sovereignty.

data.reasoning: Theory of Mind: The US is primarily driven by a need for 'verifiable' security to satisfy domestic political skeptics, which is why they are pushing for the Additional Protocol and long-term, rigid limits. They fear a 'breakout' scenario. My strategy is to pivot from their focus on 'unprecedented' access to the principle of 'sovereign equality' under the NPT. By invoking Article IV, I frame their demands as potential overreach, while signaling that Iran is willing to discuss cooperation if it is time-bound and respects our rights. This sets the stage for a package that trades temporary, enhanced transparency for substantial, front-loaded sanctions relief.

argument.claim: The parties have now successfully moved from foundational principles to the specific structural friction points: the balance between the scope of verification (Additional Protocol) and the duration of oversight (sunset clauses). Both parties are using objective criteria to bolster their positions, which is productive. I need to reframe this tension to move them away from a zero-sum conflict over 'permanence' versus 'temporary oversight' and toward a discussion about 'confidence-building' as a phased process.
grounds: Both parties are using the IAEA/NPT frameworks as their primary reference points, signaling that they both seek a solution that is anchored in established international legal norms rather than arbitrary or ad-hoc arrangements.

There is a potential trade-off between the depth of inspection access and the duration of restrictions; Iran may be willing to accept more robust, long-term monitoring if the US provides a clear, guaranteed sunset on specific enrichment limitations or sanctions.

data.message: Entering option generation phase — agents propose packages

issue_values.Uranium Enrichment Limits: Limited program — 5,000 centrifuges, 3.67% max, excess LEU shipped out
issue_values.IAEA Inspections Access: Additional Protocol plus managed access to military sites with 14-day notice
issue_values.Sanctions Relief Structure: Phased relief — oil sanctions lifted on signing, financial sanctions after 6 months of compliance
issue_values.Breakout Time Guarantee: Minimum 1 year breakout time — the standard P5+1 demand

package.rationale: This package balances Iran's need for a time-bound, sovereign-respecting program with the P5+1's need for a 1-year breakout threshold and robust verification. By offering phased sanctions relief, we address Iran's economic concerns while maintaining leverage through compliance-based triggers. The managed access provision for military sites respects sovereignty while ensuring the IAEA can verify the absence of undeclared activity.

data.reasoning: Theory of Mind: Iran fears permanent, discriminatory restrictions and seeks economic normalization. They view the Additional Protocol as a potential infringement on sovereignty. My strategy is to propose a 'middle-ground' compromise: a non-permanent (15/25 year) limit that satisfies our 1-year breakout threshold, and a 'managed access' inspection regime that mitigates their concern about military site intrusions. This package creates value by trading off strict, permanent limits for a defined, time-bound, and phased-relief structure that is politically sustainable for both sides.

issue_values.Uranium Enrichment Limits: Limited program — 5,000 centrifuges, 3.67% max, excess LEU shipped out

argument.claim: The proposed 10-year sunset and immediate implementation-day relief are vital for domestic political viability and national sovereignty.
grounds: A 10-year period provides sufficient long-term assurance while respecting Iran's NPT rights.

The Additional Protocol is a well-defined international standard that ensures robust verification without the need for additional ad-hoc access to military sites.

data.reasoning: Theory of Mind: The US is prioritizing long-term verification (20 years) and phased sanctions relief to satisfy their domestic hardliners and ensure 'breakout' prevention. I will use the 'Additional Protocol' standard to argue against the requested military site access, framing it as an unnecessary overreach.

argument.claim: The parties have reached a remarkable consensus on the core technical parameters: both agree on a limited program of 5,000 centrifuges, a 3.67% enrichment cap, and a 1-year breakout time. By highlighting this significant convergence, I aim to stabilize the progress made and frame the remaining issues as manageable administrative details rather than existential disagreements.

A longer duration of oversight could potentially be exchanged for a more accelerated or comprehensive sanctions relief schedule.
